chunks.title: Saved YouTube Video
chunks.text:
[0:04] What's up geeks and welcome to the channel! Have you ever wondered what happens during
[0:10] a game's loading screen and why some of them take so much time?
[0:14] To answer these questions, suppose you are a game
[0:16] developer working on one of these loading screens for a AAA game
[0:20] Let's think together what is the first thing that should happen for the game to start up
[0:26] Well similarly to any other application our data, our code, and the images and videos used
[0:31] have to be loaded from the local storage into the random access memory
[0:36] then because these games tend to be huge applications
[0:39] they have to create way more objects than your average app
[0:43] hence the creation of these objects will take more time compared to some normal application
[0:48] Now on top of these initialization steps some games or huge apps
[0:52] may need to download external data such as sounds, arts, translations, or many others
[0:57] and this also adds to the time of loading Additionally after doing all these steps
[1:02] some cleaning may be required to delete temporary downloads

[0:52] may need to download external data such as sounds, arts, translations, or many others
[0:57] and this also adds to the time of loading Additionally after doing all these steps
[1:02] some cleaning may be required to delete temporary downloads
[1:06] or some files that were loaded at the start just so others could
[1:10] load but now are no longer required And finally this screen may need to
[1:14] load previously saved profiles by the user or initiate a new game profile or whatever.
[1:20] Okay now why am I telling you all this? and what does that have to do with today's topic?
[1:25] You see the pieces of code I was writing since the beginning
[1:29] can actually be put inside a method and this method can be used by the
[1:33] developer working on the loading screen So if we were developing the loading
[1:38] screen of World of Warcraft for example this method will be housed inside a
[1:42] WorldOfWarcraftLoaderClass and when we need to load the game
[1:46] this class will be instantiated and the load method will be called
[1:50] Now few weeks later the manager asks you to develop the loading screen of Diablo also

[1:46] this class will be instantiated and the load method will be called
[1:50] Now few weeks later the manager asks you to develop the loading screen of Diablo also
[1:56] but while developing you noticed that both classes share the same exact steps
[2:01] However some of their implementations may vary an example could be that while the code
[2:06] for creating and loading the various needed objects was entirely different
[2:10] in both cases the code behind the deleting and the cleaning of temporary files was almost identical
[2:15] wouldn't it be great to get rid of the code duplication all-while leaving the steps and
[2:20] their order inside the algorithm structure intact? Well the Template Method Pattern suggests that
[2:27] you break down your algorithm into a series of steps or methods
[2:31] then what you have to do is put a series of calls to these methods or
[2:36] steps inside a single template method which in our case is the loadMethod,
[2:41] the steps may either be abstract or have some default implementation inside the parent class
[2:47] Now to use the algorithm defined by the series of steps the client must provide its own sub-class,

chunks.title: Saved YouTube Video
chunks.text:
[2:41] the steps may either be abstract or have some default implementation inside the parent class
[2:47] Now to use the algorithm defined by the series of steps the client must provide its own sub-class,
[2:53] implement all abstract steps, and if need be
[2:57] overwrite some of the optional ones but not the template method itself.
[3:02] Let's see how this will play out with our game loading classes.
[3:05] The first thing we need to do is extract each logic into a separate method
[3:09] in this example we can have the loadLocalData, the createObjects, the downloadAdditionalFiles,
[3:15] the cleanTempFiles, and initializeProfiles methods Now we can choose to declare all these methods
[3:23] or steps as abstract hence forcing the sub-classes created to implement all of them
[3:28] or we can choose to provide some of these methods with a default implementation in the base class
[3:34] this will allow us to get rid of code duplication inside the sub-classes we are going to create.
[3:39] The next step is to create a specific or concrete class for each loader we need
[3:45] and let it extend the BaseGameLoader class we just created

[3:55] these classes will share the same implementation of the clean method for example
[4:00] but they will provide their own specific implementation for all remaining steps
[4:05] by doing this, each step will contain the actual behavior needed by this specific game
[4:12] we are trying to load. You see that's what
[4:15] the template method is all about this pattern is a behavioral one that defines
[4:20] the skeleton of an algorithm in the superclass but lets subclasses override specific steps of the
[4:28] algorithm without changing its structure. Let's go ahead now and take a look at the
[4:33] structure or class diagram of the template method design pattern
[4:37] while taking into consideration the example and the classes we created

[4:28] algorithm without changing its structure. Let's go ahead now and take a look at the
[4:33] structure or class diagram of the template method design pattern
[4:37] while taking into consideration the example and the classes we created
[4:41] in our game loading application. The first thing you may have noticed
[4:45] is the abstract class which in our case was the BaseGameLoader class
[4:50] This class declares methods that act as steps of an algorithm
[4:54] as well as the actual template method which calls these methods in a specific order
[4:59] the steps may either be declared abstract or have some default implementation
[5:03] The second part of the diagram are the concrete classes of course
[5:07] which were represented by the WorldOfWarcraftLoader and the DiabloLoader classes
[5:13] these classes can override all of the steps provided by their parents class
[5:17] or only the abstract ones except for the template method itself.
[5:22] Okay to sum everything up, the template method pattern
[5:25] allows you to turn an algorithm into a series of individual methods

Let's go ahead now and take a look at the [4:33] structure or class diagram of the template method design pattern [4:37] while taking into consideration the example and the classes we created [4:41] in our game loading application....

chunks.title: Saved YouTube Video
chunks.text:
[5:17] or only the abstract ones except for the template method itself.
[5:22] Okay to sum everything up, the template method pattern
[5:25] allows you to turn an algorithm into a series of individual methods
[5:30] that can be easily extended and overridden by subclasses
[5:34] this is done while keeping intact the structure of your base algorithm defined in the superclass
[5:40] when you turn such an algorithm into a template method
[5:43] you will be able to pull up the steps with similar implementations into the
[5:47] superclass housing this template method hence eliminating code duplication
[5:52] Additionally the code that varies between our different implementations
[5:56] can and should remain in the sub-classes. So that's it for this video, I hope it was
[6:02] helpful thank you guys for watching, take care and I will see you in the next one!
